Placer C-ounty C-ounsel
175 FIILITIEII,ER A\'ENl]E, ROOM I]O1
AUBURN, Cr\IIFORNL{ 956O3-&581 o - COUNTY ADMINISTRATT\'E CENTER
(916) 8894044
March 1, 1990
Cathy Donovan
Fair Politica1 Practices
Commission, Legal Division
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 9581-4
Re: Request For Advice, Conflict of Interest Issue
Dear Cathy:
As the County Counsel for Placer County, it 1s my
responsibility to a&vise County government on procedures to be
foliowed in tie adoption of geneial plan documents. An issue has
arisen in our Count| regarding the participation in general plan
hearings of public oftiiials 
who may own stock in a local
telephone company, whose service arLa includes territory within
the inbit of 6t"-of tn" general plans under consideration' 
You
may recal1 that we discuised this issue in your.office in L989.
Oui office requests written assistance on this issue.
ISSUE:
May two members of the Placer County Planning^Commission,
and one elected number of the Board of Supervisors of Placer
County, participate and vote in public hearings on the adoption
of the Oiy Creek West Placer Community PIan, if they.each own
stock in Lne Roseville Telephone compaDy, which provides service
to areas covered by the proposed plan?
II{FORITATION PROVTDED :
For the benefit of your staff, w€ are providing the
following documents on this issue:
1-. A current draft of the proposal community plan.
2. The most recently published annual report of the
Roseville Telephone Company.
3. Current copies of the Form T2L filed by each public
official 
in question, supplied by our local filing officer.
.-'
=+aI


we also enclose correspondence from a local community
group, questioning the possibility of a conflict of interest,
coltlrEilTs:
we offer the follo!,ring connents as an opinion on the
question submitted.
I. 
IIIE COII}IT'NITY PI"AI{ IS A GEI'ERAL PI,ANTITrG TX'CT'IIENT VIIIH
ITO DIRECI FIIINICIAL BFTECT O AITT SPECIFIC PRO]ECI OR
PROFOSAL.
Placer county first 
adopted a county-wide General Plan in
L967. since that time, the diversity of the county led to the
developr0ent of 20 localized Community P1ans. These docunents are
prepared by pLanning professionals and consultants, and then are
the subject of a series of workshops and public hearings, as well
as environnental review, to obtain public input into the planning
process .
This plan is I'the official statenent of the county of
Placer setting forth goals, policies, and inpletnentation
proposals that hrill quide developnent in the area to at least the
year 2000.tt The plan nust be consistent with the County-Wide
General PIan, and is simpty nore geographically specific. 
(See
DRAFT DRY CREEK WEST PI,ACER COUUI,,NITY PI,AN, pages 1 - 8 . )
Landowners frequentl-y attenpt to develop property that is
not classified for developnent in a general plan or community
pLan. They are then required to apply for a general plan
amendment, for the specific real property they hrish to develop.
Such action is quite distinct fron the general plan process, and
nay indeed result in a direct financial effect.
The City of RosevilIe had sirnilar difficulties with
ownership of Roseville Telephone Company stock in 1988, however,
the planning activities under consideration there invotved
specific devel,opment proposals for specific parcels of land with
well defined custoner potential for the telephone company. That
process raises clearly a different issue than the plater- County
progran of general plan developnent. For the City the process
created a clear nexus bethreen the proposed development projects
and the direct financiaf effect on the telephone -ompany. -for
the county, the issue is sinply the general politicai c6ncept of
grol^rth .
This distinction is- most eloguentl-y stated in the January
Ll , L990, letter (enclosed) from the !{est placer Citizens,




Conrnittee, rrrhere that group attempts to paraphrase the FPPC
advice to the City of Roseville:
rrlt has also cone to our attention that the Fair
Political Practices Commission ruled that city
and county officials 
orrning more than 91,000 in
telephone conpany stock hrere ineligib)-e to vote
on major growth issues.tr (Paragraph 4, final
sentence . )
We believe this is a reference to the October 6, 1988,
advice letter No. I-88-31,5 to tlichael F. Dean, city Attorney of
Rosevilfe. The vagueness of the discussj.on in that letter led to
a dialogue on the possibili.ty of cJ.arifying the regulations.
some effort was made to rnodify Section 18703 regarding the
rrEffect on the Public Generallyrr provisions of your regulations.
It is apparent that a point of clarity has still 
not been reached
in defining the gap betlreen specific development proposals and
generaL grorrth policies for purposes of determining conflicts of
interest.
II. 
THB GMIERAL ISSUE OT GROT*TI{ POLICY IIUST BE DISTINGUISHED
rROI{ COI{FLICTS RETATED T\O FITANCIAL IXTERESTS III SPECIFIC
DEVET{'PilETT PROPOSALS.
The argument for attempting to find a conflict of interest
in a general grow'th policy is set forth again in the letter
enclosed, in paragraph 5:
rrlf extensive growth in the area is allowed,
company stockholders will benefit financially
by the creation of the increased custoners. rt
It is not difficult 
to see how this sane reasoning could
be applied to virtually 
any business within and near a planning
area. Since growth of an area will forseeably increase the
custorner base of any local business, this reasoning process would
presume to exclude anyone who profits fron a loca1 business
enterprise from participating in local government planning
activities. 
It would effectively disenfranchise a major iegrment
of our population frorn public office.
- 
FPPC regulations focus on material and specific financial
effects of the decision naking process. we hav6 reviewed section
18702 and La7o2-L with respect to nateriality. 
while it is clear
that each individual's financial interest in Rosevirre Terephone
must be exarnined for financial significance at specified
thresholds, the regulations also speak to the direct




involvement of the business entity in the decision before the
agency :
'rAny business entity . 
.in which the official has
a direct or indirect investment . . is directly
involved in a decision before the official's
agencyr,. 18702.L (2).
where the governnental decision before the agency does not
involve a grant of specific developnent rights, permits or
license to any project, individual or business entity, the mere
fact that one possible outcome of the decision process nay be
general growth of the area is certainly not a naterial financial
effect within this definition. There have been no allegations of
real property ownership by Roseville Telephone in this project
are, nor any proposal for developoent by the company.
Even assurning that financial benefits nay foreseeably flow
from generaL grohrth of an area to the loca1 business conmunity
and property owners, that does not give rise to a policy of
exclusion of all such beneficiaries from participation in the
political process. Nor can such an argument be lirnited to
beneficial effects. The regulations are clearly worded to
include effects that r0ay be detrirnental to the financial
interests of participants. Thus if anti-growth advocates are
rnotivated by concern for the effect of general qrowth policies on
property values within and adjacent to the planning area, they
also r^rould be excluded fron participation in the political
process by virtue of land ownership. one must hronder who would
be Left to represent the area in shaping its growth future.
III. 
@ilTIUilITY PI.AXS ATD OryflM. GETERAL PIAil DOCT'IiET:rS EFFECI
TIIB PT'BLTC GMERAT,LY.
our professional planners have indicated to us that
aLthough the pLanning process has been divided into local
community plans, none of them stand independent of the
County-wide ceneral p1an, of which they are essentially
cooponents. In practical effect, each conmunity plan conponent
affects the whole, and all portions of the County are impicted by
general planning policies adopted for each connunity of the
county. For exanple, the development of industrial resources in
one plan area of the County will inpact on housing planning for
many other plan areas.
Section 18703 sets forth a regutation calling for
exanination of the effect of undefined types of gov6rnrnental
decisions on the interests of the puutic-6rri"iai paiiicipi"t.
It does allow that connon effects rnay be felt by ri signiii-ant




segrment of the public,rr but also excludes an industry, trade or
profession unless the official is appointed specifically by a
Iegislative scheme to represent that group. In our examination
of Form 72L as subnj.tted by the nenobers of our Planning
Cornmission and the elected menbers of the Board of Supervisors,
rre find a wide variety of interests in businesses, including:
real estate developers, nedical/dental professj-onal offices,
liquor stores, local banks, agriculturaL operations, title
cornpanies, loca} savings and loan companies, etc.
While all officials are advised to disclose and withdra!,
fron participation in pernits and project approvals involving
financiaL interests and transactj-ons to which they may be
personally a party, there is no blanket prohibition against any
class or profession fron being actively involved in general
policymaking functions of their elected or appointed office.
(see 64 ops.cal.Atty.Gen. 795). we likewise can see no
distinction for prohibiting persons who own businesses or stock
in loca1 businesses fron participating in such general planning
activity, so long as they scrupulously abstain from decisions
involving specific projects or applications affecting their own
financial interests.
REOUEST:
we understand that this request rnay be approaching a more
substantive and less rninisterial application of your reg'ulations.
we are, hovirever, faced with hearinqs j.n nrid to late April, 1990 ,
on this conmunity Plan. It hrould be appreciated if you could
provide written advice on your position prior to April 13 if
possible. we have attenpted to provide as conplete a package and
interpretative position as possible to assist you. We r^rill" be
happy to meet r^rith you or discuss any questions by telephone.
Thank you for your consideration of this matter.
Sincerely,
eJKil,tt:
RTCHARD K. DENHALTER,
County Counsel

Enc losures

JAf,i i ;; i:,.,
PLANNING DIVISiCI.J
D irector He id i Tschud in
Conmun ity oeve loPment DePt.
Planning D iv is ion
11414 B Ave
Auburn, CA 9 5603
lf extensive growth in the area is
financially by the creation of the
three gentlemen, who currently hold
financlally benefit by pro growth,
t{ I JAN l9e0
The west Placer citizens' conmittee (wPCC) was formed in 1987 to unify and
represent the citizens of the Dry Creek - West Placer conmunity Plan area and
to function as an advocate on issues impacting anyone within the above area.
Participation and involvement in the WPCC has been and will continue to be
open and on a voluntary basis. As with any citizens' grouP, not every citizen
will choose to partieipate or to be rePresented by that group' By virtue of
WPCC quest ionna ires and resu I ts obta ined from respondents, however ' i t
appears that the WPCC does in fact represent the majority for the area'
During the Placer County Planning Conmission hearing of 13 December 1989 on
the Diy Creek - west Placer Conmunity Plan, it became apparent that Planning
Conmissioner, Jack Lish, was inclined to disPute the authenticity of the WPCC
formed within his area of responsibility. Since its formation, Mr Lish has
attended meet ings conducted by the WPCC on major issues impacting the area'
Mr Lish's actions on lS December 1989 elucidate the fact that Mr Lish is not
norprobablyhasnotbeenintouchwiththeSpecialproblemsandconcernSof
his own const ituencY.
It has come to our attention that state laws prohibit government officials
from taking part in decisions affecting their finances, property or family
members. lt has also come to our attention that the state Fair Political
Practices cormission ruled that city or county officials owning more than
$l,OOO in tetephone company stock were ineligible to vote on major growth
issues.
our research of Placer county Election Division records indicates Mr Lish does
in fact own between $1,000 and $10,000 of conmon stock in the Boseville
Telephone Company (Form 121, 'State of Economic lnterest" dated I February
1989). ln addition to Planning Conmissioner Lish, Supervisor Robert lMahan
owns Roseville Telephone Company cormon stock valued at over $100,000
receiving a return on investment of over $10,000 (per Form 721, "Statement of
Economic lnterest" dated 22 February '1989) and Mr Boss Riolo, Director,
District 1 of the Placer County Water Agency receives income ot between $l,000
and $10,000 from the Placer County Water Agency (per Form 730, "Statement of
Economic lnterest for Designated Employees" dated 24 February 1989).
allowed, company stockholders will benefit
increased customers. As a result these
dec is ion mak ing pos it ions, cou ld
h igh dens ity deve lopment dec is ion.


ln view of the foregoing, we herBby request that an investigation be performed
relative to a potential conflict of interest involving the aforement ioned
gent lemen and that they also be dismissed from any hearings or involvement in
any decision making process as it may relate to the Dry Creek - West Placer
Conmun ity Plan.
- Respectful_ly, -,,/.

NOE O. F IERROS
Core Conm ittee Member
West P lacer Citizens'Conmittee
( 916) 771-0689
